name: Changing Woman
description: Changing Woman, or Asdzáá Nádleehé in Navajo, is a central figure in Navajo mythology and one of the most important deities. She embodies the cyclical nature of life, renewal, and the passage of seasons. Born from the earth and sky, she ages from infancy to old age and then rejuvenates, constantly renewing herself and the earth along with her. She is the mother of the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer and Child-Born-of-Water, who rid the world of monsters, making it safe for humans. Changing Woman is associated with fertility, abundance, and the provision of sustenance. She is closely linked to the earth and its resources, and she taught the Navajo people how to cultivate corn and other crops. Her cyclical nature represents the importance of balance and harmony in the world. She resides in a Hogan made of turquoise in the Western Sea and is often associated with the colors turquoise, white shell, abalone, and jet. She is revered for her wisdom, kindness, and her ability to bring prosperity and wellbeing to the Navajo people. She represents the ideal of womanhood and motherhood in Navajo culture, embodying strength, resilience, and nurturing. Her stories emphasize the importance of living in harmony with nature and respecting the cyclical rhythms of life. She is considered a benevolent and powerful force, constantly working to ensure the wellbeing of her people and the health of the earth.
subtitle: The embodiment of cyclical renewal and life.

keyMyths.title: Birth of the Hero Twins
keyMyths.description: Changing Woman miraculously conceived and gave birth to the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer and Child-Born-of-Water, who were destined to rid the world of monsters.

keyMyths.title: Creation of the Navajo People
keyMyths.description: Changing Woman, using flakes of her own skin, created the first Navajo people, ensuring their survival and prosperity on the earth.

keyMyths.title: The Cyclical Nature
keyMyths.description: Changing Woman's ability to age and then rejuvenate embodies the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, reflecting the changing seasons and the renewal of the earth.

sources.author: Paul G. Zolbrod

sources.description: A retelling of the Navajo creation story.
sources.text: A retelling of the Navajo creation story.
